title: FinOps & Cost Engineering for SAP
description: FinOps is the practice of bringing financial accountability to cloud spending. This module covers HANA Cloud cost modeling (EUR/GB/hr, vCPU/hr, storage EUR/TB/hr), BTP compute sizing and right-sizing, build versus buy TCO frameworks using NPV and IRR, capacity planning with peak-to-average ratio analysis, SAP license management (indirect access, digital access, consumption-based licensing), and cost optimization patterns (right-sizing, autoscaling, spot instances). Staff+ engineers treat cloud costs as a first-order architectural constraint.

sections.title: HANA Cloud Cost Modeling — EUR/GB/hr, vCPU/hr, Storage

description.value:
HANA Cloud cost modeling is the foundation of SAP FinOps. Unlike traditional on-premise where hardware costs were a capital expense amortized over 3-5 years, HANA Cloud is an operational expense with per-hour, per-GB costs that vary by service tier, storage class, and data transfer. Understanding this cost model is essential for Staff+ engineers who must architect cost-efficient solutions and answer the inevitable interview question: "How would you reduce SAP cloud costs by 30%?"

HANA Cloud Pricing Components: HANA Cloud costs are composed of: (1) Compute (vCPU/hr): Each HANA instance has a defined number of vCPUs, and you are billed per vCPU per hour. The price per vCPU varies by tier — High Memory (for SAP BW, data-intensive workloads) is more expensive than Compute Optimized (for transactional workloads). The Staff+ insight is that vCPU allocation is not just about peak workload — HANA licenses memory per vCPU proportionally. Reducing vCPUs reduces both compute and memory cost. (2) Storage (EUR/GB/hr or EUR/TB/hr): HANA stores data in-memory plus persistent storage. The in-memory storage cost is included in the compute tier, but additional persistent storage (for logs, backups, and data lake files) is billed separately. The per-GB cost of HANA Cloud storage is typically 3-5x cheaper than standard cloud block storage because it uses the cloud provider's object storage with a caching layer. (3) Data Transfer: Egress costs (data leaving the HANA Cloud instance to on-premise or other cloud regions) are often overlooked. In a typical SAP landscape, data flows from HANA Cloud to on-premise for reporting, replication, or disaster recovery backups — these egress costs can account for 10-20% of the total bill. (4) Backup Storage: HANA Cloud automatically creates backups every 15 minutes, and these are stored for 14 days by default (configurable up to 30 days). Backup storage is billed at a lower rate than live storage but can accumulate significantly for large databases.

Right-Sizing HANA Cloud: The most common FinOps mistake is over-provisioning HANA Cloud instances. The baseline sizing recommendation from SAP is often conservative (designed for worst-case scenarios), but most workloads have a peak-to-average ratio of 2:1 to 5:1, meaning the instance is idle for 50-80% of the time. Right-sizing uses historical workload data (from HANA workload management or ST03N) to find the optimal instance size. The process: (1) collect CPU and memory utilization over 30 days, (2) identify the p95 utilization (the value that 95% of samples fall below), (3) size the instance to the p95 value with a 20% headroom buffer, (4) downsize any instance where the p95 utilization is below 50% of allocated capacity. For HANA Cloud, you can change the instance size without downtime if using the HANA Cloud scalability feature (auto-scaling with a minimum and maximum size).

* HANA Cloud cost tracking (retrieve billing data via API)
* SAP BTP provides Usage Metering API (JSON)
* URI: /usage/v2/applications/{app}/metrics

CLASS zcl_hana_cost_model DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: calculate_instance_cost
      IMPORTING
        iv_vcpus       TYPE i
        iv_hours       TYPE i
        iv_tier        TYPE string
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_cost) TYPE decfloat16,
      calculate_storage_cost
        IMPORTING
          iv_gb          TYPE i
          iv_hours       TYPE i
        RETURNING
          VALUE(rv_cost) TYPE decfloat16.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_hana_cost_model IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD calculate_instance_cost.
    DATA(lv_rate) = SWITCH #( iv_tier
      WHEN 'compute'    THEN '0.8'
      WHEN 'highmem'    THEN '2.4'
      WHEN 'storageopt' THEN '1.2' ).
    rv_cost = iv_vcpus * iv_hours * CONV decfloat16( lv_rate ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD calculate_storage_cost.
    " EUR 0.04/GB/hr for standard tier
    rv_cost = iv_gb * iv_hours * CONV decfloat16( '0.04' ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Example: Right-sizing analysis report
* 30-day utilization from HANA workload management
* Query: M_WORKLOAD (system view)
SELECT TO_DECIMAL(
    AVG(CPU_UTILIZATION), 5, 2 ) AS p95_cpu
  FROM M_WORKLOAD_REPORT
  WHERE TIMESTAMP > ADD_DAYS( CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, -30 )
    AND SERVICE_NAME = 'indexserver'
  INTO @DATA(lv_p95_cpu).

* Rule: p95 < 50% -> candidate for downsizing
* Monitor with: /nDBACOCKPIT -> HANA -> Workload

decisionTree.title: Choosing the Right HANA Cloud Service Tier
decisionTree.prompt: Should I use Compute Optimized, High Memory, or Storage Optimized HANA Cloud for this workload?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "What is the ratio of in-memory data size to CPU cores needed?" If the workload is transactional with moderate data (data < 1TB), use Compute Optimized. If it is analytical with large in-memory datasets (data > 2TB), use High Memory. If it is a data lake scenario with warm/cold tiering, use Storage Optimized.

branches.condition: Transactional OLTP, < 1TB data, high transaction throughput needed
branches.path: Compute Optimized. Best EUR/vCPU ratio. Ideal for: S/4HANA transactional, Fiori app backends, real-time order processing. Typical: 16-32 vCPU, 4-8 TB storage.

branches.condition: Analytical / BW workload, > 2TB in-memory data, complex aggregations
branches.path: High Memory. Highest memory-to-vCPU ratio. Essential for: SAP BW/4HANA, large financial consolidations, real-time analytics on full dataset. Expect 4x cost vs Compute Optimized.

branches.condition: Warm / cold data with infrequent queries, large historical datasets
branches.path: Storage Optimized via HANA NSE (Native Storage Extension). Hot data in memory, warm data on SSD with caching. Cost: ~10x cheaper than keeping all data in memory. Use for: historical reporting, audit data, archival.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The key FinOps insight is that HANA pricing is dominated by memory, not CPU. For analytical workloads, 70% of the cost is memory. NSE (Native Storage Extension) can reduce memory cost by 60% by moving cold data to disk. Always model TCO with and without NSE before choosing a tier.

description.value:
SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) offers multiple runtimes for running ABAP and non-ABAP workloads: Cloud Foundry (CF) for microservices, ABAP Environment (also known as Steampunk) for ABAP Cloud, Kyma for Kubernetes-based workloads, and the Neo environment (legacy). Each runtime has a different cost model, scaling behavior, and sizing strategy. Staff+ engineers must understand how to size these runtimes to minimize cost while maintaining SLO compliance.

ABAP Environment (Steampunk) Sizing: ABAP Environment uses the ABAP Compute Unit (ACU) as the sizing unit. Each ACU includes a defined amount of CPU, memory, and disk. The recommended sizing for a production ABAP Environment system is typically 2-4 ACUs minimum, with additional ACUs for each additional 100 concurrent users or 10,000 background job executions per day. However, these guidelines are conservative — actual utilization data should drive sizing decisions. ABAP Environment supports autoscaling: you configure minimum and maximum ACUs, and the platform scales between them based on CPU utilization. The key cost optimization is to set the minimum ACU to the p50 utilization (not the p95) and allow autoscaling to cover peaks. This can reduce baseline cost by 30-40% compared to a fixed-size deployment.

Cloud Foundry Sizing: Cloud Foundry applications are sized in memory units (MB/GB) with a disk allocation. The standard recommendation is to start with 256MB-1GB per application instance and scale horizontally. The cost optimization pattern is right-sizing each application based on its actual memory utilization — many BTP CF applications use less than 100MB but are allocated 1GB. Use the Cloud Foundry CLI (cf app APP_NAME) to check the actual memory usage and downsize accordingly. For stateful applications, use at least 2 instances for high availability; for stateless applications, use a minimum of 2 instances spread across availability zones. The CF pricing model charges per GB of memory per hour, so reducing memory allocation by 50% directly reduces cost by 50%.

Kyma (Kubernetes) Sizing: Kyma clusters are billed per worker node (vCPU + memory). The sizing strategy is to use smaller nodes (e.g., 2 vCPU, 8GB RAM) and scale horizontally, rather than large nodes (8 vCPU, 32GB RAM) with low utilization. Use Kubernetes Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) to right-size pod requests and limits, and Cluster Autoscaler to add/remove nodes based on actual demand. Kyma costs are dominated by the control plane (free for most tiers) and the worker nodes. The FinOps pattern for Kyma is to use spot/preemptible instances for non-production and batch workloads, which reduce compute costs by 60-80%.

* ABAP Environment (Steampunk) sizing — ACU model
* 1 ACU ~ 2 vCPU + 8 GB RAM + 30 GB disk
* Production: min 2 ACUs, max 10 ACUs (with autoscaling)
* Development: 1-2 ACUs fixed (no scaling needed)

CLASS zcl_btp_sizing DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: estimate_acu
      IMPORTING
        iv_concurrent_users TYPE i
        iv_daily_jobs       TYPE i
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_acus)      TYPE i,
      estimate_cf_memory
        IMPORTING
          iv_instances       TYPE i
          iv_memory_per_gb   TYPE decfloat16
        RETURNING
          VALUE(rv_cost_day) TYPE decfloat16.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_btp_sizing IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD estimate_acu.
    rv_acus = 2. " baseline
    rv_acus = rv_acus + ( iv_concurrent_users DIV 100 ).
    rv_acus = rv_acus + ( iv_daily_jobs DIV 10000 ).
    rv_acus = nmin( val1 = rv_acus val2 = 10 ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD estimate_cf_memory.
    DATA(lv_rate) = CONV decfloat16( '0.02' ). " EUR/GB/hr
    rv_cost_day = iv_instances * iv_memory_per_gb
      * lv_rate * 24.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Cloud Foundry: check actual memory usage
* cf app my-app
* Look for: memory: 512M  (actual usage: 85M)
* In this case, downsize to 256M allocation
* Save ~50% on memory cost

* Kyma: use spot instances
* In Kyma cluster configuration:
* spec:
*   provider:
*     type: aws
*     machineType:
*       spot: true
*       preset: performance
* Reduces worker node cost by ~70%

organizationalDimension.title: Building a FinOps Culture in SAP Engineering Teams
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: The 'Cost Per Transaction' model: Phase 1 assigns a EUR/transaction cost to every major business process (sales order, purchase order, invoice). Phase 2 shows each team their monthly cost footprint and trends. Phase 3 ties team-level OKRs to cost efficiency (reduce cost/transaction by 10% YoY). Phase 4 implements cost gates in CI/CD — any deployment that increases estimated cost/transaction by >5% requires architecture review.

stakeholders.concern: Cost tracking is overhead that slows development
stakeholders.address: Cost visibility is automatic (from BTP usage metering API) — no manual entry. Engineers see their cost dashboard once per sprint in the retro. No form-filling required.

stakeholders.concern: Cost efficiency OKRs conflict with feature velocity OKRs
stakeholders.address: Cost and velocity are not in tension — inefficient architecture slows both. A badly sized HANA instance costs $50K/month AND runs slower than an optimally sized one. Cost optimization IS velocity optimization.

stakeholders.concern: We need predictable budgeting, and variable cloud costs make that hard
stakeholders.address: Use reserved instances for baseline load (60-70% of capacity, fixed cost) and on-demand/spot for the remainder (variable). This gives 80% cost predictability while maintaining flexibility. The cost model becomes: fixed reserved + variable usage.

organizationalDimension.adoptionPhases: Phase 1: Assign EUR/transaction costs. Phase 2: Team-level cost dashboards. Phase 3: Cost efficiency OKRs. Phase 4: CI/CD cost gates.
organizationalDimension.metrics: Month 3: 80% of teams have cost dashboards configured. Month 6: 3 teams reduce cost/transaction by 15%. Month 12: 20% overall cost reduction from FinOps culture alone (no architectural changes).
organizationalDimension.antiPatterns: (1) Cost visibility only for leadership — teams must see their own costs to change behavior. (2) Treating all costs as variable — reserve 60-70% baseline for predictability. (3) Cost optimization done once (e.g., yearly right-sizing exercise) instead of continuous.

sections.title: Build vs. Buy TCO Framework — NPV, IRR & Total Cost of Ownership

description.value:
The build vs. buy decision is one of the most common and consequential architecture decisions in enterprise SAP. Should you build a custom extension on BTP, buy an ISV solution from SAP Store, or extend the standard S/4HANA using SAP's built-in extensibility? The answer is not technical — it is financial. A Staff+ engineer uses financial frameworks (Total Cost of Ownership, Net Present Value, Internal Rate of Return) to quantify the trade-offs and present a data-driven recommendation to leadership.

Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Framework: TCO captures all costs over the system's lifetime, not just the initial development or license fee. The TCO equation includes: (1) Initial costs: Development labor (build) or license/onboarding fee (buy), infrastructure setup, data migration, integration testing. (2) Recurring costs: Infrastructure (HANA Cloud, BTP runtime), maintenance (support contract or internal maintenance team), operations (monitoring, backup, incident response), training (user onboarding, documentation). (3) End-of-life costs: Data migration to replacement, decommissioning, archival. For a typical enterprise SAP extension with a 5-year lifespan, the initial costs are 20-30% of the total, and the recurring costs are 70-80% — meaning a cheaper initial build can be more expensive over the long term if it has higher maintenance costs.

Net Present Value (NPV): NPV discounts future cash flows to present value using a discount rate (typically the company's Weighted Average Cost of Capital, WACC, usually 8-15%). NPV = Sum of (Cash Flow in Year N) / (1 + Discount Rate)^N over the project lifespan. If NPV(build) > NPV(buy), building is financially preferable. The discount rate reflects the risk: build projects typically use a higher discount rate (+2-5%) because of execution risk (the build may be delayed, over-budget, or fail to meet requirements). This higher discount rate reduces the NPV of the build option, making buy more attractive when risk is high.

Internal Rate of Return (IRR): IRR is the discount rate at which NPV = 0. It represents the annualized return of the investment. If the IRR exceeds the company's hurdle rate (minimum acceptable return, typically 15-20% for IT projects), the investment is approved. For build vs. buy, the project with the higher IRR is preferred. Example: building an extension costs EUR 500K initially with EUR 100K/year maintenance, while buying costs EUR 200K/year subscription. The IRR of buying is effectively infinite (no upfront cost), while building may have an IRR of 25% — but the risk-adjusted IRR (accounting for execution risk) may be only 12%, below the hurdle rate, making buy the better decision.

* TCO calculator for SAP build vs buy
CLASS zcl_tco_calculator DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: calculate_npv
      IMPORTING
        iv_initial_cost   TYPE decfloat16
        iv_recurring_cost TYPE decfloat16
        iv_discount_rate  TYPE decfloat16
        iv_years          TYPE i
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_npv)     TYPE decfloat16,
      compare_build_vs_buy
        IMPORTING
          is_build TYPE ty_project
          is_buy   TYPE ty_project
        RETURNING
          VALUE(rv_recommendation) TYPE string.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_tco_calculator IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD calculate_npv.
    DATA(lv_total) = iv_initial_cost.
    DO iv_years TIMES.
      DATA(lv_year) = sy-index.
      DATA(lv_discounted) = iv_recurring_cost
        / ( 1 + iv_discount_rate ) ** lv_year.
      lv_total = lv_total + lv_discounted.
    ENDDO.
    rv_npv = lv_total.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD compare_build_vs_buy.
    " Build: initial cost higher, recurring lower
    " Buy: initial cost lower, recurring higher
    DATA(lv_build_npv) = calculate_npv(
      iv_initial_cost   = is_build-initial
      iv_recurring_cost = is_build-recurring_per_year
      iv_discount_rate  = is_build-discount_rate
      iv_years          = is_build-years ).

    DATA(lv_buy_npv) = calculate_npv(
      iv_initial_cost   = is_buy-initial
      iv_recurring_cost = is_buy-recurring_per_year
      iv_discount_rate  = is_buy-discount_rate
      iv_years          = is_buy-years ).

    IF lv_build_npv < lv_buy_npv.
      rv_recommendation = 'BUILD recommended'.
    ELSE.
      rv_recommendation = 'BUY recommended'.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Example:
* Build: EUR 500K initial, EUR 80K/year, 12% discount, 5 years
* Buy:  EUR 50K initial, EUR 200K/year, 10% discount, 5 years
* Build NPV = 500K + 80K/1.12 + 80K/1.12^2 + ... = ~EUR 788K
* Buy  NPV = 50K + 200K/1.10 + 200K/1.10^2 + ... = ~EUR 808K
* Build is EUR 20K cheaper over 5 years (financially better)

sections.title: Capacity Planning — Peak-to-Average Ratio & Predictive Scaling

description.value:
Capacity planning for SAP landscapes is notoriously difficult because SAP workloads are bursty and seasonal. Month-end closing, quarter-end financial reporting, and year-end consolidation create demand spikes that can be 3-10x the average load. Capacity planning is the art of sizing infrastructure to handle these peaks without over-provisioning for the 90% of the time when demand is low. The key metric is the peak-to-average ratio (P2A), and the key strategy is predictive scaling.

Peak-to-Average Ratio Analysis: P2A is calculated as (peak demand) / (average demand) over a defined period (typically 30 days). A P2A of 3 means the peak is 3x the average — if you size for the peak, you waste 66% of capacity during low demand. For SAP batch workloads, P2A can be 10-15x during month-end. For online (dialog) workloads, P2A is typically 1.5-3x. The FinOps strategy is: (1) for dialog workloads, size for p95 (the value exceeded only 5% of the time), because a slight degradation during the top 5% peak is acceptable. (2) For batch workloads, implement job scheduling to smooth the demand curve — spread month-end jobs across the last 3 days instead of the last day. (3) For the remaining peak, use autoscaling or burst capacity.

SAP Predictive Scaling: SAP BTP and HANA Cloud support predictive scaling based on calendar-based schedules. For example: scale up HANA Cloud to 8 ACUs on the last 3 days of each month (month-end closing), scale down to 3 ACUs the rest of the month. This reduces cost by approximately 50% compared to a fixed 8-ACU deployment. The scaling schedule is configured in the BTP Cockpit or HANA Cloud Central. The schedule must be aligned with the business calendar — consider public holidays, regional variations (different month-end for different countries), and fiscal year-end (often a different month than calendar year-end). The Staff+ approach is to build a capacity model that blends predictive scaling for known patterns with reactive scaling (based on CPU/memory thresholds) for unexpected spikes.

y1="180" x2="110" y2="210" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="270" y1="180" x2="270" y2="210" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="430" y1="180" x2="430" y2="210" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="590" y1="180" x2="590" y2="210" class="sys-edge"/><rect class="hi-edge" x="100" y="210" width="500" height="40"/><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="230">Calendar-Based Predictive Scaling Schedule</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="245">Normal: 3 ACU | Month-end: 8 ACU | Quarter-end: 12 ACU</text><line x1="350" y1="250" x2="350" y2="275" class="sys-edge"/><rect class="sys-box" x="40" y="275" width="195" height="40"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="137" y="292">Cost: Fixed 8 ACU = EUR X/mo</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="137" y="305">Predictive: avg 4 ACU = 50% savings</text><rect class="sys-box" x="252" y="275" width="195" height="40"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="292">Reservation: 1-year commit</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="305">30-40% discount vs on-demand</text><rect class="sys-box" x="465" y="275" width="195" height="40"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="562" y="292">Spot: Batch + non-prod</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="562" y="305">60-80% discount</text><line x1="137" y1="315" x2="137" y2="345" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="350" y1="315" x2="350" y2="345" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="562" y1="315" x2="562" y2="345" class="sys-edge"/><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="365">Goal: reduce P2A via scheduling + absorb remaining with scaling</text></svg>

* Predictive scaling schedule for HANA Cloud
* Configure via SQL or HANA Cloud Central UI

ALTER SYSTEM SCALING SCHEDULE CREATE
  NAME 'MonthEnd_ScaleUp'
  FREQUENCY MONTHLY
  OCCURRENCE 1
  TIME '2025-01-29 06:00:00'
  ACTION SCALE_UP
  TARGET_COMPUTE 8
  DESCRIPTION 'Month-end closing - increase compute'
  ENABLED TRUE;

ALTER SYSTEM SCALING SCHEDULE CREATE
  NAME 'MonthEnd_ScaleDown'
  FREQUENCY MONTHLY
  OCCURRENCE 1
  TIME '2025-02-01 22:00:00'
  ACTION SCALE_DOWN
  TARGET_COMPUTE 3
  DESCRIPTION 'Month-end complete - reduce compute'
  ENABLED TRUE;

* Monitor scaling events:
SELECT * FROM M_SCALING_EVENTS
  WHERE TIMESTAMP > ADD_DAYS( CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, -30 )
  ORDER BY TIMESTAMP DESC;

* ABAP: capacity utilization reporting
* Report: Z_CAPACITY_UTILIZATION (run from SM36)
SELECT mandt, progname, jobcount,
       AVG( runtime_sec ) AS avg_runtime,
       MAX( runtime_sec ) AS peak_runtime,
       SUM( runtime_sec ) AS total_runtime
  FROM zjob_statistics
  WHERE jobdate BETWEEN @lv_start AND @lv_end
  GROUP BY mandt, progname
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_job_stats).

* Identify peak days: run per-day aggregate
* Build schedule that distributes jobs across low-utilization windows

adversarialResponse.title: Handling 'Your capacity planning model always over-provisions. We are wasting 40% on idle compute.'
adversarialResponse.challenge: "Your predictive scaling adds 20% headroom on top of p95. That means we are paying for 20% capacity we never use. Why not just use reactive autoscaling and pay only for what we use?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "The 20% headroom is standard practice. Without it, you risk performance degradation during unexpected spikes." (defensive, dismisses valid cost concern)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You are right — 20% headroom on p95 means 20% over-provisioning during normal operation. Here is why I recommend it: autoscaling latency for HANA Cloud is 5-15 minutes. During those 5-15 minutes, the system is under-provisioned. For transactional workloads, this means users experience >2s response times, which triggers business complaints. The cost of 20% headroom ($4K/month for a 16-vCPU instance) vs the cost of 15 minutes of degraded user experience during a spike ($50K in lost productivity if 1000 users are affected) — the headroom is 12x cheaper. However, I agree we can optimize: for non-production systems, set headroom to 0% and use reactive autoscaling only. For production batch windows (which are predictable), use predictive scaling without headroom and keep reactive autoscaling as a safety net. For production dialog (unpredictable), keep the 20% headroom. This tiered approach saves ~15% vs flat 20% headroom across all systems."
adversarialResponse.pattern: acknowledge-and-tier — acknowledge the cost concern is valid, explain WHY the headroom exists (autoscaling latency vs user impact), then propose a tiered approach that differentiates by workload type. The Staff+ signal is not defending a fixed rule — it is adapting the rule to the risk profile of each workload.

sections.title: License Management — Indirect Access, Digital Access & Consumption

description.value:
SAP license management is one of the highest-risk and highest-cost areas for large enterprises. The transition from traditional user-based licensing (named user, professional, limited professional) to consumption-based models (Digital Access, SAP BTP Consumption Credits) has fundamentally changed how companies must manage their SAP costs. A Staff+ engineer must understand license models because architectural decisions (API design, integration patterns, system landscape) directly affect license costs.

Indirect Access (Classic Model): Indirect access occurs when a non-SAP system (a third-party application, a custom-built portal, a legacy system) reads from or writes to SAP data without a licensed SAP user. SAP has historically pursued indirect access audits aggressively, claiming that any interaction with SAP data requires a license for the underlying user. The 2018 SAP-Independent Software Vendor (ISV) settlement clarified some rules, but the risk remains. The defense is architectural: (1) Use SAP API Management as a proxy — all external calls go through the API gateway, which logs the source system for audit purposes. (2) Use the Digital Access model (document-based) instead of user-based licensing for external integrations. (3) For every integration, classify the interaction: is it a human-initiated transaction (requires named user license) or a system-to-system document exchange (use Digital Access)?

Digital Access Model: Introduced in 2018, Digital Access replaces indirect access licensing with a document-based model. You license based on the number of core business documents (sales orders, purchase orders, invoices, etc.) processed by external systems. Each document type has a defined count (e.g., one sales order = one document). The cost per document varies by document type and volume tier. The advantage is predictability: you know the cost per document, whereas Indirect Access had unpredictable audit risk. The disadvantage is that high-volume, low-value document processing (e.g., IoT sensor data creating 1M documents/month) can be very expensive under Digital Access. The architectural mitigation is to consolidate documents: batch 100 sensor events into one document instead of creating 100 separate documents.

BTP Consumption Credits: BTP offers a consumption-based model where you purchase credits (with a 12-month expiry) and consume them across BTP services (Integration Suite, Extension Suite, Analytics, etc.). The rate of consumption depends on the service and the volume. The Staff+ strategy is to monitor consumption rates monthly and adjust commitments quarterly. BTP Consumption Credits can be a trap: if you do not monitor consumption, you may exhaust credits before the year ends and face expensive overage charges (typically 2-3x the committed rate). Automated monitoring and alerting (via the BTP Usage Dashboard API) is essential.

* Document counting for Digital Access licensing
CLASS zcl_digital_access_counter DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: count_document
      IMPORTING
        iv_doc_type TYPE string
        iv_source   TYPE string,
      get_license_consumption
        RETURNING VALUE(rt_consumption) TYPE zda_consumption_tab.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mt_docs TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zda_log.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_digital_access_counter IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD count_document.
    APPEND VALUE #(
      doc_type  = iv_doc_type
      source    = iv_source
      timestamp = cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( )
      counter   = 1
    ) TO mt_docs.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_license_consumption.
    SELECT doc_type, COUNT(*) AS total
      FROM @mt_docs AS docs
      GROUP BY doc_type
      INTO TABLE @rt_consumption.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Usage: count every external document creation
zcl_digital_access_counter=>count_document(
  iv_doc_type = 'SALES_ORDER'
  iv_source   = 'EXTERNAL_PORTAL' ).

* Monthly license report for SAP compliance audit
* Use transaction SLG1 to log all external document access
* Run Z_LICENSE_AUDIT_REPORT monthly:
*   - List all systems that accessed SAP data
*   - Classify each as: Named User / Digital Access / Indirect
*   - Identify unlicensed access
*   - Report to license management team for remediation

* BTP Consumption Credit monitoring
* API: /usage/v2/applications/{app}/metrics
* Automate check: if remaining credits < 20% of monthly burn,
*   alert procurement to renegotiate or purchase more credits
* Key metric: burn rate (credits consumed / days elapsed)
*   If burn rate > (total credits / 365), will exhaust early

sections.title: Cost Optimization Patterns — Right-Sizing, Autoscaling, Spot

description.value:
Cost optimization in SAP cloud landscapes is a continuous process, not a one-time exercise. The cloud cost flywheel — measure, optimize, repeat — applies to SAP as much as to any cloud-native workload. The patterns below are proven strategies that FAANG engineers apply to their own SAP-like workloads at scale. Each pattern has a cost impact, an implementation effort, and a risk profile that must be evaluated.

Pattern 1: Right-Sizing (highest impact, lowest risk): Right-sizing is the single most effective cost optimization for SAP. As discussed in the HANA Cloud cost modeling section, most SAP instances are over-provisioned by 30-50%. The process: (1) collect 30 days of utilization data (CPU, memory, I/O, network), (2) identify the p95 utilization for each metric, (3) resize to match p95 + 20% buffer, (4) repeat quarterly. Right-sizing reduces compute cost proportionally — downsizing from 8 vCPU to 4 vCPU reduces compute cost by 50%. The risk is sizing too aggressively — if the p95 grows and the instance becomes CPU-bound, user experience degrades. Mitigate by setting up monitoring alerts at 80% utilization to detect growth trends before they cause performance issues.

Pattern 2: Autoscaling (high impact, medium risk): HANA Cloud and BTP support autoscaling based on CPU utilization or schedule. HANA Cloud autoscaling allows you to set a minimum (for baseline) and maximum (for peak) number of ACUs. The instance scales up when CPU exceeds a threshold (e.g., 70%) and scales down when CPU drops below a threshold (e.g., 30%). The risk is scale-up latency — a scale-up can take 5-15 minutes, during which the instance is under-provisioned. Mitigate by using predictive scaling for known peaks and autoscaling only for unexpected spikes. The cost savings come from running at the minimum capacity most of the time.

Pattern 3: Spot and Preemptible Instances (medium impact, medium risk): For non-production systems (development, test, sandbox) and batch workloads that can be interrupted and restarted, use spot instances (AWS, Azure, GCP). Spot instances are 60-80% cheaper than on-demand but can be terminated with 30-second notice. SAP batch jobs should be designed to be idempotent and restartable. For HANA Cloud, spot instance support is limited; however, for BTP Cloud Foundry and Kyma, spot instances for worker nodes are available. The pattern is to run all non-production systems on spot and use on-demand only for production.

Pattern 4: Reserved Instances and Savings Plans (high impact, low risk): If you have predictable, steady-state SAP workloads (e.g., production S/4HANA running 24/7), commit to 1-year or 3-year Reserved Instances (RIs) or Savings Plans (SPs). RIs provide a 30-50% discount over on-demand pricing. The Staff+ challenge is forecasting future usage accurately enough to commit without waste. Start with a conservative reservation covering 60-70% of the expected baseline — cover the remaining 30-40% with on-demand or spot. Review and adjust the reservation coverage quarterly.

-> Optimize -> Monitor -> Repeat (quarterly)</text></svg>

* Cost optimization dashboard data collection
CLASS zcl_cost_optimizer DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: collect_metrics,
      suggest_rightsizing
        RETURNING VALUE(rt_suggestions) TYPE zopt_suggestion_tab.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_cost_optimizer IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD collect_metrics.
    " Collect CPU, memory, I/O from HANA system views
    INSERT INTO zopt_metrics
      SELECT * FROM M_HOST_RESOURCE_UTILIZATION
        WHERE TIMESTAMP > ADD_DAYS( CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, -1 ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD suggest_rightsizing.
    " Analyze 30-day p95 and suggest optimal size
    SELECT metric, AVG( value ) AS avg_val
      INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_totals)
      FROM zopt_metrics
      WHERE timestamp > ADD_DAYS( CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, -30 ).

    " For each instance, compare current size vs p95 + 20%
    " Suggest downsizing if current > p95 + 20%
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Monthly cost report (run from SM36 as Z_COST_OPT_REPORT)
* Output: chart of savings by optimization pattern
* Example results:
*   Right-sizing:      EUR 12,500/mo saved (3 instances downsized)
*   Reserved Instances: EUR 8,200/mo saved (70% coverage)
*   Autoscaling:        EUR 4,300/mo saved (4 ACU avg vs 8 fixed)
*   Spot Instances:     EUR 2,100/mo saved (6 dev/test instances)
*   Total savings:      EUR 27,100/mo (32% of current spend)

* Alert on cost anomaly
* If monthly spend > 1.3x rolling average -> page FinOps team
* Check for: new environment, data transfer spike, misconfiguration

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Reserved Instances vs On-Demand for HANA Cloud

variables.name: Peak load (month-end)
variables.source: During financial close, last 3 days of month

variables.name: Annual on-demand cost
variables.source: HANA Cloud list pricing, Compute Optimized tier

cp.monthlyPenalty: All on-demand: $240K/year. Cost: highest unit price. Benefit: maximum flexibility — can downsize anytime. Best for: variable workloads, short-term projects, dev/test environments.

ap.monthlyPenalty: Reserved baseline + on-demand peak: reserve 8 ACU baseline at 30% discount ($17.50/hr = $153K/year), pay on-demand for 16 ACU during 72hr month-end ($28,800). Total: $182K/year. Savings: $58K/year (24%).

calculation.breakeven: Reserved instances break even vs on-demand at 4+ months of continuous use per year. For HANA production systems that run 24/7/365, 1-year reserved is 30% cheaper. 3-year reserved is 60% cheaper. Recommend: reserve 70% of baseline for 3 years, leave 30% on-demand for flexibility.

details: Staff+ Insight: FinOps expertise is increasingly valued in FAANG interviews because cloud cost management is a universal concern. At AWS, Meta, and Google, the ability to design cost-efficient architectures is part of every SDE and TPM interview rubric. For SAP engineers, FinOps knowledge demonstrates that you think beyond functional requirements to operational and financial constraints. The ability to articulate NPV, TCO, and capacity planning principles in an SAP context positions you as a strategic engineer who understands business impact, not just technical implementation.
